query: most common site of esophageal carcinoma global incidence

retrieved_docs.title: Esophageal Cancer -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: In the US, esophageal cancers represent the sixth most common gastrointestinal cancer, with 20,640 new cases per year, and are the eighth most common cancer worldwide. The highest-risk area, called the “esophageal cancer belt,” includes portions of northern Iran, southern Russia, central Asian countries, and northern China, where SCC dominates all cases by 90%. In this risk area, esophageal cancer is the fourth most common cause of cancer.

retrieved_docs.title: Esophageal Cancer -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: In recent decades, particularly in Western countries, including the US, the incidence of esophageal SCC has decreased. Conversely, the incidence of esophageal adenocarcinoma has rapidly increased during the same period in the Western world, paralleling the rise of GERD in the general population, with a notable association for patients with a high body mass index. [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Esophageal Cancer -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Esophageal cancers are grouped based on histologic features encompassing squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) and adenocarcinoma. In the past 3 decades, SCC has been decreasing while adenocarcinoma has been increasing in incidence in the United States (US). However, when stratified by anatomical location, the incidence of adenocarcinoma of the distal esophagus and gastroesophageal junction (GEJ) is actually increasing due to the Barrett esophagus. In the US, gastroesophageal cancers represent the fourth most common gastrointestinal cancer after colorectal, pancreatic, and hepatobiliary cancers and have the third-highest mortality rate. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Esophageal Ulcer -- Epidemiology -- Malignancy-Related Ulcers
retrieved_docs.content: Although esophageal SCC is the most prevalent worldwide, the incidence of adenocarcinoma is on the rise. Adenocarcinoma is now the most prevalent esophageal cancer histology in the United States and Western Europe, notably affecting White men. The rise in the incidence of adenocarcinoma is currently unknown. Still, it may be related to obesity and GERD, as this histology is more commonly seen in the distal esophagus, with Barrett esophagus as the precursor lesion. (National Cancer Institute Esophageal Cancer Treatment)

retrieved_docs.title: Esophageal Cancer -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Location Tumors in the upper and middle thirds of the esophagus tend to have a worse prognosis compared to those in the lower third or esophagogastric junction due to the greater likelihood of presenting at an advanced stage and the technical challenges associated with surgical resection.

retrieved_docs.title: Esophageal Cancer -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Esophageal SCC is associated with low economic status and lack of refrigeration. As global economies have become wealthier, the adoption of refrigeration for meat storage has increased, while the use of meat curing and associated nitrosamine exposure has decreased. This shift has contributed to a reduced incidence of esophageal SCC worldwide.

retrieved_docs.title: Esophageal Cancer -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Esophageal SCC is more likely to occur in Black patients. The primary risk factors include cigarette smoking and excessive alcohol consumption, as well as achalasia, caustic injury, tylosis, and Plummer-Vinson syndrome. Additionally, HPV has been correlated with an increased incidence of SCC in the upper cervical esophagus. [5] Patients with esophageal SCC are at risk for synchronous or metachronous head and neck or lung SCC.

retrieved_docs.title: Esophageal Cancer -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Histologic subtype Squamous cell carcinoma: Generally has a worse prognosis compared to adenocarcinoma, particularly in Western countries, due to the higher likelihood of presenting at an advanced stage and the association with smoking and alcohol consumption. Adenocarcinoma: Has a slightly better prognosis compared to SCC, particularly when diagnosed at an early stage. However, the incidence of adenocarcinoma has been rising in recent years, and it now accounts for the majority of esophageal cancer cases in Western countries.

retrieved_docs.title: Esophageal Cancer -- Pearls and Other Issues -- Additional Considerations of Esophageal Cancer Management
retrieved_docs.content: Understanding the different histologic subtypes of esophageal cancer, such as SCC and adenocarcinoma, is essential for treatment planning. While SCC is more common in developing nations, adenocarcinoma incidence is rapidly rising in Western countries. Accurate staging, using the TNM classification system, is critical for guiding treatment decisions and predicting outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Esophageal Ulcer -- Prognosis -- Malignancy-Related Ulcers
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis of esophageal cancer is largely determined by the stage at diagnosis. Early detection and the extent of surgical resection are key factors for a more favorable outcome. Esophageal cancer is generally incurable but treatable. According to the National Cancer Institute, the 5-year relative survival rate for esophageal cancer is 21.6%. Patients diagnosed at an early, localized stage have a more favorable prognosis, with a 5-year relative survival rate of 48.1%. (National Cancer Institute: SEER Cancer Stat Facts: Esophageal Cancer. Bethesda, Md: National Cancer Institute. https://seer.cancer.gov/statfacts/html/esoph.html )

retrieved_docs.title: Esophageal Ulcer -- Epidemiology -- Malignancy-Related Ulcers
retrieved_docs.content: The incidence of esophageal cancer has been increasing worldwide over the past few decades. According to the recent American Cancer Society data, esophageal cancer occurs predominantly in men. In 2024, the incidence of esophageal cancer in men was 17,690 compared to 4680 in women. Associated mortality was reported in 12,880 men and 3250 women. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Esophageal Cancer -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Esophageal cancer is classified into 2 main types based on histologic features: squamous cell carcinoma and adenocarcinoma. In recent decades, the incidence of esophageal squamous cell carcinoma has decreased. In contrast, esophageal adenocarcinoma has risen significantly in the United States, particularly in the distal esophagus and gastroesophageal junction, mainly due to the prevalence of Barrett's esophagus.

retrieved_docs.title: Esophageal Cancer -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Esophageal cancer can lead to numerous complications that profoundly affect both the quality of life and survival of patients. These complications can arise from the primary tumor's growth, the spread of metastatic disease, or as a consequence of the various treatments administered to manage the cancer.

retrieved_docs.title: Esophageal Cancer -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Stage at diagnosis Localized (Stage 0-I): 5-year survival rate of 45% to 50% Regional (Stage II-III): 5-year survival rate of 20% to 30% Distant (Stage IV): 5-year survival rate of <5% [28] [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Esophageal Leiomyoma -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: There is a higher incidence of leiomyoma of the esophagus in men when compared to women; this is in the order of 2:1. The most frequent occurrence is usually between the ages of 20 and 50 years. The most typical location of these tumors is in the lower two-thirds of the esophagus. The occurrence of these tumors in the upper one-third of the esophagus accounts for only 10% of all leiomyomas of the esophagus. Most tumors are detected when they are less than 5 cm in size. Rarely do they grow larger than 10 cm, and then they are called giant leiomyoma of the esophagus. These may occur as single or multiple tumors. [5] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Esophageal Stricture -- Etiology -- Malignant Stricture
retrieved_docs.content: Esophageal adenocarcinoma Esophageal squamous cell carcinoma Metastatic esophageal neoplasm - usually from lung cancer

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 1135
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of the esophagus, cancer of the head and neck, cancer of other gastrointestinal organs—including the peritoneum—and cancer of other urinary organs are all associated with ورم خبيث, indicating that each of these neoplasms is a malignant tumor. Recognizing that these diverse anatomical sites share the characteristic of malignancy underscores the need for oncologic assessment and coordinated management across these organ systems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2711
retrieved_docs.content: Endoscopic dilation of esophagus is linked to the procedural category upper gastrointestinal endoscopy; biopsy, indicating that it is considered an alternative or related mapping of that endoscopic examination. Upper gastrointestinal endoscopy with biopsy is classified under the same category of upper gastrointestinal endoscopy; biopsy, reflecting its role as a diagnostic approach that includes tissue sampling. Together, these statements illustrate that both the therapeutic Endoscopic dilation of esophagus and the diagnostic Upper gastrointestinal endoscopy with biopsy belong to the broader domain of upper gastrointestinal endoscopic procedures, highlighting their complementary roles in evaluating and managing esophageal pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 878
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of the bladder, cancer of the esophagus, cancer of the head and neck, cancer of other gastrointestinal organs – including the peritoneum, and cancer of other urinary organs are all associated with ورم خبيث, indicating that each of these conditions represents a malignant tumor. Recognizing that these diverse anatomical sites share the characteristic of malignancy underscores the need for oncologic evaluation, appropriate staging, and coordinated treatment strategies across these cancer types.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 4 -- Community 2914
retrieved_docs.content: Esophageal disorders are classified under the category “اضطرابات مَريئِيّة أخرى” and are clinically associated with “تعاطي الكحول” (alcohol consumption), indicating that alcohol use is a relevant factor in the development or exacerbation of these esophageal conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1800
retrieved_docs.content: The medical classification framework designates “esophageal disorders” as belonging to the category “اضطرابات مَريئِيّة أخرى” (other esophageal disorders), indicating that these conditions are grouped under a broader label of miscellaneous esophageal pathology. In addition, the term “Without” is noted to be associated with esophageal disorders, reflecting that the absence of specific clinical features or findings is a relevant descriptor when evaluating or documenting these gastrointestinal conditions. This relationship underscores the importance of both categorical classification and descriptive terminology in the assessment and management of esophageal disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2274
retrieved_docs.content: Alcoholic cirrhosis (تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ) is a specific subtype of liver cirrhosis (تَشَمُّع كَبِدي). Liver cirrhosis, in turn, is clinically associated with esophageal variceal bleeding (نزيف دَوالِي المريء). Recognizing that patients with alcoholic cirrhosis develop the broader condition of liver cirrhosis helps clinicians anticipate the high risk of variceal hemorrhage, prompting appropriate screening, monitoring, and preventive interventions to improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2825
retrieved_docs.content: Other gastrointestinal diagnostic procedures encompass a range of examinations used to evaluate the digestive tract, and within this broader group, التنظير الداخلي للجهاز الهضمي (gastrointestinal endoscopy) serves as a parent category for specific techniques such as تنظير مريئي (esophageal endoscopy). Thus, تنظير مريئي is classified as a subtype of التنظير الداخلي للجهاز الهضمي and is included among other gastrointestinal diagnostic procedures, highlighting its role in the systematic assessment of esophageal pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2883
retrieved_docs.content: The therapeutic procedures specific to the esophagus (الإجراءات العلاجية الخاصة بالمريء) include esophageal stenting (مفاغرة مريئية) as one of their components, indicating that مفاغرة مريئية is a particular type of الإجراء العلاجي المتعلق بالمريء.

The knowledge base therefore integrates congenital correction, diagnostic imaging, endoscopic therapeutic techniques, variceal pathophysiology, and related hepatic risk factors, emphasizing the interconnected role of vascular morphology, procedural categories, and clinical associations in the management of esophageal and related gastrointestinal diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 1176
retrieved_docs.content: Gastrointestinal hemorrhage is classified under upper gastrointestinal bleeding (نَزِيف الجزء العلوي من الجهاز الهضمي) and is frequently associated with variceal bleeding (نزيف الدوالي الوريدية). One of the key manifestations of gastrointestinal hemorrhage is the presence of esophageal varices (دوالي المَريء), which arise in the esophageal plexus (مفاغرة مريئية). Recognizing that esophageal varices represent a specific source of bleeding within the broader category of upper gastrointestinal hemorrhage helps clinicians target diagnostic evaluation and therapeutic interventions, such as endoscopic control of variceal bleeding, to improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1516
retrieved_docs.content: The entity “Diagnosis/Diseases Component” is classified as a sub‑element of both “اضطراب في عنق الرحم” (cervical disorder) and “اضْطِرابُ المريء” (esophageal disorder), indicating that this diagnostic component is relevant to the evaluation and categorization of diseases affecting the cervix as well as the esophagus. This dual parentage reflects its role in the clinical assessment of disorders in these distinct anatomical regions.
